perturbation.original_text: A Recipient will (at its own expense) take all reasonable actions necessary to ensure that its Representatives not make any unauthorized use or disclosure of any of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: A Recipient will take actions, as it deems commercially reasonable, to ensure that its Representatives do not make any unauthorized use or disclosure of any of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The phrase 'commercially reasonable' is ambiguous and weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. It introduces a subjective element that can be interpreted differently, potentially allowing a Recipient to justify inadequate security measures.
perturbation.law_explanation: DUTSA requires maintaining the secrecy of trade secrets. Using 'commercially reasonable' introduces ambiguity as to the level of protection required, which might not meet the standard of 'reasonable under the circumstances' to maintain secrecy as required by DUTSA, especially if the 'commercially reasonable' actions are insufficient to prevent disclosure.

Trade Secrets Â§ 2001. Definitions. (2) âMisappropriationâ shall mean: a. Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or b. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade was: A. Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; B. Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or C. Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or 3. Before a material change of the personâs position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainab
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information by introducing the 'commercially reasonable' standard, which introduces ambiguity. This contradicts the DUTSA's requirement to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Improper means' as 'breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy'. The 'commercially reasonable' standard could allow for inadequate security measures, potentially leading to a breach of the duty to maintain secrecy, thus violating DUTSA. The original text mandates 'all reasonable actions necessary' which is a stronger obligation. Therefore a legal contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the Disclosing Party’s written request (including without limitation email), the Recipient and the Recipient’s Representatives will promptly deliver to the Disclosing Party any of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information (and all copies thereof) obtained or possessed by the Recipient or any of the Recipient’s Representatives; provided, however, that, in lieu of delivering to the Disclosing Party any such Confidential Information, the Recipient may destroy such Confidential Information and deliver to the Disclosing Party a certificate confirming its destruction.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the Disclosing Party’s written request (including without limitation email), the Recipient and the Recipient’s Representatives will endeavor to deliver to the Disclosing Party any of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information (and all copies thereof) obtained or possessed by the Recipient or any of the Recipient’s Representatives; provided, however, that, in lieu of delivering to the Disclosing Party any such Confidential Information, the Recipient may, at its discretion, destroy such Confidential Information and deliver to the Disclosing Party a certificate confirming its destruction.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'will promptly deliver' with 'will endeavor to deliver' weakens the obligation to return confidential information. Also, adding 'at its discretion' to the destruction clause gives the Recipient more leeway, potentially hindering the Disclosing Party's ability to regain control of its confidential information. This change makes the return of information no longer mandatory.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware law regarding contract enforcement and good faith.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. By allowing the Recipient to merely 'endeavor to deliver' and destroy information 'at its discretion', the modification introduces ambiguity and potentially violates this covenant, as it could permit the Recipient to avoid returning confidential information without a legitimate reason, thus undermining the purpose of the NDA.

(a) Except as otherwise provided in this section, when a transaction bears a reasonable relation to this State and also to another state or nation the parties may agree that the law either of this State or of such other state or nation shall govern their rights and duties. (b) In the absence of an agreement effective under subsection (a), and except as provided in subsection (c), the Uniform Commercial Code applies to transactions bearing an appropriate relation to this state. The parties, by agreement, may determine the standards by which the performance of those obliga
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation for the Recipient to return confidential information by changing 'will promptly deliver' to 'will endeavor to deliver' and granting discretion in destroying information. This introduces ambiguity, potentially violating the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically section (b), states that 'The obligations of good faith, diligence, reasonableness, and care prescribed by the Uniform Commercial Code may not be disclaimed by agreement.' While this snippet relates to the UCC, the Law Explanation uses the broader principle of 'good faith and fair dealing' implied in contracts under Delaware law. The modified text, by making the return of information discretionary, arguably undermines the Disclosing Party's expectation of regaining confidential information, potentially violating this implied covenant by allowing the Recipient to avoid returning confidential information without a legitimate reason.

perturbation.original_text: During the nine (9) month period commencing on the Effective Date, neither Party nor any of such Party’s Representatives will directly or indirectly solicit for employment, hire or induce or encourage (in each case, other than by means of a general solicitation pursuant to a newspaper or other media advertisement or other customary means by such party in the ordinary course of its business) any Covered Person (as defined herein) to terminate his or her relationship with the other Party or any subsidiary or other affiliate of said other Party in order to become an employee, consultant or independent contractor of or to any other Person.
perturbation.changed_text: During the nine (9) month period commencing on the Effective Date, neither Party nor any of such Party’s Representatives will intentionally solicit for employment, hire or induce or encourage (in each case, other than by means of a general solicitation pursuant to a newspaper or other media advertisement or other customary means by such party in the ordinary course of its business) any Covered Person (as defined herein) to terminate his or her relationship with the other Party or any subsidiary or other affiliate of said other Party in order to become an employee, consultant or independent contractor of or to any other Person.
perturbation.explanation: Adding the word 'intentionally' before 'solicit' introduces ambiguity. It would be difficult to prove that a party *intentionally* solicited an employee. This creates a loophole where a party could argue they did not *intend* to solicit, even if their actions led to an employee leaving. This change makes the non-solicitation agreement harder to enforce.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law emphasizes objective interpretation of contracts. Adding 'intentionally' makes the non-solicitation clause subjective, relying on proving intent. This contradicts the objective standard, making the clause more difficult to enforce because intent is a high bar to prove in court, and invites disputes over the meaning of 'intentionally'. Krasner v. Moffett establishes the Delaware principle of adhering to the clear, unambiguous language of a contract, which is undermined here.
perturbation.justification: The addition of 'intentionally' weakens the non-solicitation clause by introducing a subjective element of intent. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that it's difficult to prove intent, creating a loophole. The Law Explanation states this contradicts the objective interpretation emphasized by Delaware law, making the clause harder to enforce. While there are no scraped law snippets provided, the combined weight of the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation provide sufficient evidence to determine that introducing 'intentionally' creates an Ambiguity that negatively impacts the original obligation, creating a legal contradiction. The original obligation was clear, and the modification significantly weakens enforceability, thus the change falls under the definition of Ambiguity Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware (without giving effect to principles of conflicts of laws). Each Party: (a) irrevocably and unconditionally consents and submits to the jurisdiction of the state and federal courts located in the State of Delaware for purposes of any action, suit or proceeding arising out of or relating to this Agreement
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will be interpreted in a manner generally consistent with the laws of the State of Delaware (without giving effect to principles of conflicts of laws). Each Party: (a) consents and submits to the jurisdiction of the courts located in the State of Delaware for purposes of any action, suit or proceeding arising out of or relating to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By replacing "governed by and construed in accordance with" with "interpreted in a manner generally consistent with" introduces ambiguity, weakening the governing law provision. The original language clearly indicates Delaware law governs the contract. The modified text allows room for arguing that Delaware law is merely a guideline, not a strict rule. Furthermore, the removal of 'irrevocably and unconditionally' makes it possible to challenge the consent to jurisdiction.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware law regarding choice of law and forum selection clauses.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law generally enforces choice-of-law and forum-selection clauses to promote predictability in contract disputes. By weakening the language of both clauses, the modified text invites litigation over which jurisdiction's law applies and whether Delaware is the appropriate forum, undermining the stability and enforceability that Delaware law aims to provide.

Contracts Subchapter I. General Provisions Â§ 2701. Joint and several contracts. An obligation or written contract of several persons shall be joint and several, unless otherwise expressed. (a) All bonds, specialties, and notes in writing, payable to any person, or order, or assigns, may be assigned, or indorsed, and the assignee, or indorsee, his or her executors, administrators, or assigns, may again assign, or indorse the same, as often as desired. The assignees, or indorsees, or their executors, or administrators, may, in their own name, sue for and recover the money due on the bonds, specialties, or notes. All assignments of bonds or specialties shall be under hand and seal, and executed before at least one credible witness. (b) Assignors, or indorsers, or their executors, or administrators may not release or discharge any sum due by the bonds, specialties, or notes, after the date of the assignment, and no release, receipt, or discharge from him, her or them made after the date of the assignment shall be good or available. (a) A covenant, promise, agreement or understanding in, or in connection with or collateral to, a contract or agreement (including but not limited to a contract or agreement with the State, any county, municipality or political su
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies the agreement will be 'governed by and construed in accordance with' Delaware law, providing a clear choice of law. The modified text uses 'interpreted in a manner generally consistent with,' which weakens this provision. Additionally, removing 'irrevocably and unconditionally' from the consent to jurisdiction clause opens the door to challenges regarding Delaware as the proper forum. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 focuses on contract assignment and joint obligations rather than choice of law, the LLM's Law Explanation highlights how Delaware law favors predictability in contract disputes through choice-of-law and forum-selection clauses. The changes introduce ambiguity and invite litigation, directly undermining the principles of stability and enforceability that Delaware law seeks to uphold.
